name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ مَآ أَحَلَّ ٱللَّهُ لَكَ ۖ تَبْتَغِى مَرْضَاتَ أَزْوَٰجِكَ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١)
```

```english
O Prophet! Why do you disallow [yourself] what Allah has made lawful for you, seeking to please your wives? And Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (1)
```

```arabic
قَدْ فَرَضَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ تَحِلَّةَ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٢)
```

```english
Allah has certainly made lawful for you the dissolution of your oaths, and Allah is your master and He is the All-knowing, the All-wise. (2)
```

```arabic
وَإِذْ أَسَرَّ ٱلنَّبِىُّ إِلَىٰ بَعْضِ أَزْوَٰجِهِۦ حَدِيثًا فَلَمَّا نَبَّأَتْ بِهِۦ وَأَظْهَرَهُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ عَرَّفَ بَعْضَهُۥ وَأَعْرَضَ عَنۢ بَعْضٍ ۖ فَلَمَّا نَبَّأَهَا بِهِۦ قَالَتْ مَنْ أَنۢبَأَكَ هَـٰذَا ۖ قَالَ نَبَّأَنِىَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ (٣)
```

```english
When the Prophet confided a matter to one of his wives, but when she divulged it [instead of guarding the secret] and Allah apprised him about it, he acquainted [her] with part of the matter and ignored part of it. So when he told her about it, she said, ‘Who informed you about it?’ He said, ‘The All-knowing and the All-aware has informed me.’ (3)
```

```arabic
إِن تَتُوبَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُمَا ۖ وَإِن تَظَـٰهَرَا عَلَيْهِ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ مَوْلَىٰهُ وَجِبْرِيلُ وَصَـٰلِحُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۖ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ ظَهِيرٌ (٤)
```

```english
If the two of you repent to Allah... for your hearts have certainly swerved, and if you back each other against him, then [know that] Allah is indeed his guardian, and his supporters are Gabriel, the righteous among the faithful and, thereafter, the angels. (4)
```

```arabic
عَسَىٰ رَبُّهُۥٓ إِن طَلَّقَكُنَّ أَن يُبْدِلَهُۥٓ أَزْوَٰجًا خَيْرًا مِّنكُنَّ مُسْلِمَـٰتٍ مُّؤْمِنَـٰتٍ قَـٰنِتَـٰتٍ تَـٰٓئِبَـٰتٍ عَـٰبِدَٰتٍ سَـٰٓئِحَـٰتٍ ثَيِّبَـٰتٍ وَأَبْكَارًا (٥)
```

```english
It may be that if he divorces you his Lord will give him, in [your] stead, wives better than you: [such as are] muslim, faithful, obedient, penitent, devout and given to fasting, virgins and non-virgins. (5)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ قُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَأَهْلِيكُمْ نَارًا وَقُودُهَا ٱلنَّاسُ وَٱلْحِجَارَةُ عَلَيْهَا مَلَـٰٓئِكَةٌ غِلَاظٌ شِدَادٌ لَّا يَعْصُونَ ٱللَّهَ مَآ أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ مَا يُؤْمَرُونَ (٦)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Save yourselves and your families from a Fire whose fuel will be people and stones, over which are [assigned] severe and mighty angels, who do not disobey whatever Allah commands them and carry out what they are commanded. (6)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَا تَعْتَذِرُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۖ إِنَّمَا تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٧)
```

```english
[They will call out to the faithless:] ‘O faithless ones! Do not make any excuses today. You are being requited only for what you used to do.’ (7)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ تَوْبَةً نَّصُوحًا عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يُكَفِّرَ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَيُدْخِلَكُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ يَوْمَ لَا يُخْزِى ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّبِىَّ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ ۖ نُورُهُمْ يَسْعَىٰ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ أَتْمِمْ لَنَا نُورَنَا وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٨)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Repent to Allah with sincere repentance! Maybe your Lord will absolve you of your misdeeds and admit you into gardens with streams running in them, on the day when Allah will not let down the Prophet and the faithful who are with him. Their light will move swiftly before them and on their right. They will say, ‘Our Lord! Perfect our light for us, and forgive us! Indeed You have power over all things.’ (8)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ جَـٰهِدِ ٱلْكُفَّارَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٩)
```

```english
O Prophet! Wage jihad against the faithless and the hypocrites, and be severe with them. Their refuge will be hell, and it is an evil destination. (9)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah begins by referring to what transpired between the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and some of his wives regarding the story of the "prohibition" (التحريم). It admonishes the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) for prohibiting for himself what Allah had made lawful for him, seeking thereby the pleasure of some of his wives. The ultimate aim of this is an admonition of those specific wives and a defense of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), as indicated by the context of the verses.

Then, it addresses the believers, commanding them to protect themselves from the punishment of Allah—the Fire whose fuel is humans and stones—from which they shall not be recompensed except for their own deeds, and from which there is no salvation except for the Prophet and those who believe with him. Subsequently, it addresses the Prophet regarding the struggle (الجهاد) against the disbelievers and the hypocrites.

The Surah concludes by Allah setting forth an example of women for the disbelievers and an example of them for the believers.

The evidence of the context clearly indicates that the Surah is Medinan (مدنية); there is no doubt about this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكَ تَبْتَغِي مَرْضاتَ أَزْواجِكَ وَاللهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — *O Prophet! Why do you prohibit [for yourself] what Allah has made lawful for you, seeking to please your wives? And Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful.* (66:1). This is an address tinged with admonition for his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) prohibiting for himself some of what Allah had made lawful for him. The Exalted did not explicitly state what it was, nor did He clarify its nature. However, His saying: **تَبْتَغِي مَرْضاتَ أَزْواجِكَ** (*seeking to please your wives*) hints that it was a lawful action performed by the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) which his wives did not approve of. They pressured and distressed him until he sought to please them by swearing an oath to abandon it and not perform it thereafter.

Thus, His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُ** (*O Prophet*) attaches the address and the call to the description of "Prophet" (النبي) rather than "Messenger" (الرسول), because this matter pertained to his personal conduct rather than the universal message, making it more suitable than the description of messengership (الرسالة).

His saying: **لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكَ** (*Why do you prohibit what Allah has made lawful for you?*): What is meant by "prohibition" (التحريم) here is causing a state of forbiddenness through an oath (الحلف), as indicated by the following verse. The apparent meaning of His saying: **قَدْ فَرَضَ اللهُ لَكُمْ تَحِلَّةَ أَيْمانِكُمْ** (*Allah has already ordained for you the absolution of your oaths*) etc., is that he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had sworn an oath regarding that matter. It is the nature of an oath to necessitate an obligation (الوجوب) if the oath is to perform an act, and a prohibition (الحرمة) if the oath is to abstain from an act. Since he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had sworn to abstain from what Allah had made lawful for him, he had effectively "prohibited" what Allah made lawful through the oath.

The "prohibition" intended here is not his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) legislating (تشريعه) a legal forbiddenness upon himself regarding what Allah had legislated as permissible, for he does not have the authority to do that.

His saying: **تَبْتَغِي مَرْضاتَ أَزْواجِكَ** (*seeking to please your wives*), meaning you seek their pleasure through this prohibition. This phrase is either an appositive (بدل) for "you prohibit" (تحرم) etc., or a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for its subject. The sentence serves as an indication that the admonition is, in reality, directed toward the wives. This is supported by His saying when addressing two of them: **إِنْ تَتُوبا إِلَى اللهِ فَقَدْ صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُما** (*If the two of you repent to Allah... for your hearts have certainly swerved*) etc., alongside His saying: **وَاللهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** (*And Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful*).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَدْ فَرَضَ اللهُ لَكُمْ تَحِلَّةَ أَيْمانِكُمْ وَاللهُ مَوْلاكُمْ وَهُوَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ** — *Allah has already ordained for you the absolution of your oaths, and Allah is your master, and He is the All-knowing, the All-wise.* (66:2). Al-Raghib said: "Every instance where 'Allah has ordained upon him' (فرض الله عليه) appears, it refers to an obligation (الإيجاب) that Allah has imposed. Whatever appears as 'Allah has ordained for him' (فرض الله له) refers to Him not restricting it for him, such as: **ما كانَ عَلَى النَّبِيُّ مِنْ حَرَجٍ فِيما فَرَضَ اللهُ لَهُ** (*There is no blame on the Prophet in what Allah has ordained for him*) and His saying: **قَدْ فَرَضَ اللهُ لَكُمْ تَحِلَّةَ أَيْمانِكُمْ** (*Allah has already ordained for you the absolution of your oaths*)." [End quote]. The word **التحلة** (absolution) is originally *tahlulah* (تحللة), on the weight of *tadhkirah* (تذكرة) and *takrimah* (تكرمة); it is a verbal noun like *tahlil* (تحليل). Al-Raghib said: "The saying of the Mighty and Majestic: **قَدْ فَرَضَ اللهُ لَكُمْ تَحِلَّةَ أَيْمانِكُمْ** means He has clarified that by which the knot of your oaths is untied through expiation (الكفارة)."

The meaning is: Allah has decreed for you—as if He decreed it as a portion for them since He did not prevent them from untying the knot of the oath—the dissolution of your oaths through expiation. And Allah is your Guardian (المولى) — *the one who takes charge of your affairs through legislation and guidance* — and He is the All-Knowing, the All-Wise.

The verse contains an indication that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had sworn to abstain, and he was commanded to absolve his oath.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذْ أَسَرَّ النَّبِيُّ إِلى بَعْضِ أَزْواجِهِ حَدِيثاً فَلَمَّا نَبَّأَتْ بِهِ وَأَظْهَرَهُ اللهُ عَلَيْهِ عَرَّفَ بَعْضَهُ وَأَعْرَضَ عَنْ بَعْضٍ فَلَمَّا نَبَّأَها بِهِ قالَتْ مَنْ أَنْبَأَكَ هذا قالَ نَبَّأَنِيَ الْعَلِيمُ الْخَبِيرُ** — *When the Prophet confided a matter to one of his wives, but when she divulged it and Allah informed him of it, he made known part of it and passed over part of it. So when he informed her of it, she said, ‘Who informed you of this?’ He said, ‘The All-knowing and the All-aware informed me.’* (66:3). The word **السر** (secret) is the talk that you keep to yourself and hide. **الإسرار** (confiding) is your disclosing the talk to another while charging them to keep it hidden. The pronoun in "she divulged" (نَبَّأَتْ) refers to one of his wives; the pronoun in "it" (بِهِ) refers to the talk the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) confided to her; the pronoun in "informed him" (أَظْهَرَهُ) refers to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); the pronoun in "of it" (عَلَيْهِ) refers to her informing another of it and spreading the secret; the pronouns in "made known" (عَرَّفَ) and "passed over" (أَعْرَضَ) refer to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); the pronoun in "part of it" (بَعْضَهُ) refers to the talk; and the indication in "this" (هذا) refers to her informing another and spreading the secret.

The summary of the meaning is: When the Prophet disclosed to one of his wives—who was Hafsa, daughter of Umar ibn al-Khattab—a matter and charged her to keep it secret, but she informed another of it and spread the secret contrary to his instruction, and Allah informed the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that she had told another and spread the secret, he made known and informed her of part of it and overlooked another part. When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) informed her of the matter, she said to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Who informed you and told you that I told another and spread the secret?" The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "The All-Knowing, the All-Aware informed me and told me—He is Allah, the Knower of the secret and the manifest, the One Aware of the inner thoughts (السرائر)."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنْ تَتُوبا إِلَى اللهِ فقد صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُما وَإِنْ تَظاهَرا عَلَيْهِ فَإِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ مَوْلاهُ وَجِبْرِيلُ وَصالِحُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ ظَهِيرٌ** — *If the two of you repent to Allah... for your hearts have certainly swerved, and if you back each other against him, then Allah is indeed his guardian, and Gabriel, the righteous among the faithful, and the angels thereafter are [his] supporters.* (66:4). Meaning: If you two repent to Allah, then that which necessitates repentance has already occurred from you; and if you back each other against him, then Allah is his Guardian, etc.

Reports are in agreement that the two are Aisha and Hafsa, the two wives of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

The word **الصغو** (swerving) means leaning, and what is meant here is leaning toward falsehood (الباطل) and departing from uprightness. What occurred from them in terms of distressing him and backing each other against him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was among the major sins (الكبائر). The Exalted has said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ لَعَنَهُمُ اللهُ فِي الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةِ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ عَذاباً مُهِيناً** — *Indeed those who offend Allah and His Apostle are cursed by Allah in the world and the Hereafter, and He has prepared for them a humiliating punishment.* (33:57). And He said: **وَالَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ رَسُولَ اللهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ** — *...and those who offend the Apostle of Allah—for them there is a painful punishment.* (9:61).

The expression "your hearts" (قلوبكما) using the plural to intend the dual is a common usage in Arabic.

His saying: **وَإِنْ تَظاهَرا عَلَيْهِ فَإِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ مَوْلاهُ** (*and if you back each other against him, then Allah is indeed his guardian*): **التظاهر** (backing each other) means cooperation. The origin of *tazahara* (تظاهرا) is *tatazahara* (تتظاهرا). The pronoun of separation (ضمير الفصل) in His saying: **فَإِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ مَوْلاهُ** (*then Allah is indeed his guardian*) is to indicate that Allah, the Glorified, has a special concern for him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), helping him and taking charge of his affairs directly without any intermediary from His creation. The "Guardian" (المولى) is the protector (الولي) who takes charge of one's affairs and aids him against whoever intends ill for him.

"Gabriel" (جِبْرِيلُ) is coordinated with the Divine Name, and "the righteous among the faithful" (صالِحُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ) is also coordinated like Gabriel. What is meant by "the righteous among the faithful," as has been said, are the righteous ones among the believers. Thus, "the righteous" (صالح) is a singular intended as a collective, like saying: "The righteous man (الصالح) among people does not do this," meaning the genus, as in saying: "Whoever is righteous among them does not do it." Similar to this is the saying: "I was among the night-talker (السامر) and the dweller (الحاضر)."

However, this involves comparing a term annexed to a plural with a term entered by the definite article "al-"; the apparent meaning of "the righteous of the believers" (صالِحُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ) is not the same as "the righteous one from among the believers" (الصالح من المؤمنين).

Narrations have been transmitted through Sunni chains from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and through Shi'i chains from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) that what is meant by "the righteous among the faithful" is Ali (عليه أفضل السلام); this will reach you shortly, if Allah wills.

There are other opinions regarding its meaning which we have overlooked due to the lack of evidence for them.

His saying: **وَالْمَلائِكَةُ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ ظَهِيرٌ** (*and the angels thereafter are [his] supporters*): The singular form of the predicate (ظهير) is used to indicate that they are in agreement in aiding him, united as a single rank. In placing them "after that" (بَعْدَ ذلِكَ)—that is, after the guardianship of Allah, Gabriel, and the righteous among the faithful—there is an element of glorification and magnification.

The tone of the verses in manifesting the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) over those who distress him and intend ill for him, and the severity of the admonition against those who back each other against him, is remarkable. In these verses, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is addressed first and admonished for prohibiting what Allah made lawful for him, and he is directed toward the absolution of his oath; this is a manifestation, support, and victory for him, even if it appears in the form of an admonition.

Then, the address shifts from him to the believers in His saying: **وَإِذْ أَسَرَّ النَّبِيُّ إِلى بَعْضِ أَزْواجِهِ** (*When the Prophet confided a matter to one of his wives*), referring to the story while keeping it vague. He had already supported the Prophet and manifested him before referring to the story and its disclosure, which was kept sealed; in this, there is further manifestation of him.

Then, the address shifts from the believers to the two wives, affirming that their hearts had swerved because of what they had done. He did not command them to repent from their sin, but rather clarified to them that they were between two options: either they repent, or they back each other against one whose Guardian is Allah, Gabriel, the righteous among the faithful, and all the angels thereafter. Then He expressed the prospect that if he were to divorce them, Allah would provide him with wives better than them. Then He commanded the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to struggle against the disbelievers and the hypocrites and to be harsh with them.

The discourse concludes with Him, the Exalted, setting forth two examples: an example for those who disbelieve and an example for those who believe.

Allah has structured the discourse in the Surah, after addressing their situation with His saying: **إِنْ تَتُوبا إِلَى اللهِ فَقَدْ صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُما وَإِنْ تَظاهَرا عَلَيْهِ** (*If the two of you repent to Allah... for your hearts have certainly swerved, and if you back each other against him*) etc., between addressing the state of the believers and the state of the disbelievers.

He said: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا قُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ وَأَهْلِيكُمْ** (*O you who have faith! Save yourselves and your families*) etc., and **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لا تَعْتَذِرُوا** (*O you who faithless! Do not make any excuses*) etc., and He said: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا تُوبُوا** (*O you who have faith! Repent*) etc., and **يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ جاهِدِ** (*O Prophet! Wage jihad*) etc., and He said: **ضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا** (*Allah draws an example for the faithless*) and **وَضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (*And Allah draws an example for the faithful*).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **عَسى رَبُّهُ إِنْ طَلَّقَكُنَّ أَنْ يُبْدِلَهُ أَزْواجاً خَيْراً مِنْكُنَ** — *It may be that if he divorces you, his Lord will give him in your stead wives better than you...* (66:5) until the end of the verse. This is a declaration of Divine independence (استغناء إلهي); for although they were honored by the honor of being the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wives, dignity (الكرامة) with Allah is through godwariness (التقوى), as the Exalted said: **فَإِنَّ اللهَ أَعَدَّ لِلْمُحْسِناتِ مِنْكُنَّ أَجْراً عَظِيماً** — *...then Allah has indeed prepared for the virtuous among you a great reward.* (33:29). Note the position of "among you" (مِنْكُنَ). And He said: **يا نِساءَ النَّبِيِّ مَنْ يَأْتِ مِنْكُنَّ بِفاحِشَةٍ مُبَيِّنَةٍ يُضاعَفْ لَهَا الْعذابُ ضِعْفَيْنِ وَكانَ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيراً وَمَنْ يَقْنُتْ مِنْكُنَّ لِلَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَتَعْمَلْ صالِحاً نُؤْتِها أَجْرَها مَرَّتَيْنِ وَأَعْتَدْنا لَها رِزْقاً كَرِيماً** — *O wives of the Prophet! Whoever of you commits a flagrant indecency, her punishment shall be doubled, and that is easy for Allah. But whoever of you is obedient to Allah and His Apostle and acts righteously, We shall give her her reward twice over, and We have prepared for her a noble provision.* (33:30-31).

Therefore, He expressed this independence by the prospect of replacing them, if he divorced them, with wives better than them. He linked the report to the qualities of dignity mentioned for the new wives: that they be "submitting" (مسلمات), "believing" (مؤمنات), "obedient" (قانتات), "penitent" (تائبات), "devout" (عابدات), "given to fasting" (سائحات), "previously married" (ثيبات), and "virgins" (أبكارا).

Thus, whoever the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) marries who possesses the totality of these qualities would be better than them. This is only because of a specific distinction in "obedience" (القنوت) and "repentance" (التوبة), or obedience alone, while sharing with them the other qualities. Obedience (القنوت) is the persistence in obedience coupled with humility (الخضوع).

This meaning is supported by what appears in the example of Maryam (peace be upon her) at the end of the Surah regarding the mention of obedience: **وَكانَتْ مِنَ الْقانِتِينَ** (*and she was one of the obedient*). Thus, obedience is what they lack—namely, their persistence in obeying the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), which contains the obedience of Allah, and their guarding against disobeying the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and distressing him.

From what has preceded, the error of those who say that the reason for the superiority of the subsequent wives over the previous ones—if he were to divorce them—is the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) marriage to them and the separation of the previous wives, is evident. They argue that his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) marriage is an immeasurable honor.

This is because if the criterion for the "goodness" (الخير) mentioned in the verse were merely the marriage itself, then every woman the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) married would be better and more noble than them if he divorced them, even if she did not possess any of the mentioned qualities of dignity. In that case, there would be no reason to enumerate the qualities that were enumerated.

The author of *al-Kashshaf* said: "If you ask: Why were all the qualities left without a coordinating conjunction (العاطف), while one was placed between 'previously married' and 'virgins'? I say: Because they are two mutually exclusive qualities that cannot coexist in one person, unlike the other qualities." [End quote].

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا قُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ وَأَهْلِيكُمْ ناراً وَقُودُهَا النَّاسُ وَالْحِجارَةُ** — *O you who have faith! Save yourselves and your families from a Fire whose fuel is humans and stones...* (66:6). **قُوا** (*Save/Protect*) is a command from *wiqayah* (الوقاية), meaning to preserve something from what harms and hurts it. **الوقود** (*fuel*) with a *fatha* on the *waw* is a noun for that with which a fire is kindled, such as wood and the like. What is meant by the Fire is the Fire of Hell (جهنم). The fact that the humans punished therein are its fuel means that the humans themselves ignite within it, as in His saying: **ثُمَّ فِي النَّارِ يُسْجَرُونَ** (*...then they shall be kindled in the Fire*) (40:72). This is consistent with the embodiment of deeds (تجسم الأعمال), as is apparent in the following verse: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا** (*O you who disbelieve*) etc. The "stones" (الحجارة) have been interpreted as idols.

His saying: **عَلَيْها مَلائِكَةٌ غِلاظٌ شِدادٌ لا يَعْصُونَ اللهَ ما أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ** (*...over it are harsh and severe angels, who do not disobey Allah in what He commands them, and they carry out what they are commanded*). Meaning: Appointed over it to carry out various types of punishment upon its inhabitants are angels who are "harsh" (غلاظ) and "severe" (شداد).

**الغلاظ** (*harsh*) is the plural of *ghaliz* (غليظ), the opposite of gentle. What is most suitable for this context is that "harshness" refers to the roughness of action, as in His coming saying: **جاهِدِ الْكُفَّارَ وَالْمُنافِقِينَ وَاغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ** (*...wage jihad against the faithless and the hypocrites, and be harsh with them*) (66:9). **الشداد** (*severe*) is the plural of *shadid* (شديد), meaning strong in resolve and action.

His saying: **لا يَعْصُونَ اللهَ ما أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ** (*who do not disobey Allah in what He commands them, and they carry out what they are commanded*) is like an explanation for "harsh and severe." That is, they are committed to what Allah has commanded them of the types of punishment; they do not disobey Him through opposition or rejection, and they perform what they are commanded exactly as they were commanded, without anything being missed or diminished due to weakness or fatigue. Thus, they are harsh and severe.

From this, it becomes clear that His saying: **لا يَعْصُونَ اللهَ ما أَمَرَهُمْ** (*do not disobey Allah in what He commands them*) refers to their commitment to the obligation (التكليف), while His saying: **وَيَفْعَلُونَ** (*and they carry out*) etc., refers to the action according to it. Thus, there is no repetition, as some have claimed.

It is said in *al-Tafsir al-Kabir* regarding this verse: "In it is an indication that the angels are 'obligated' (مكلفون) in the Hereafter with what Allah has commanded them and what He has forbidden them, and disobedience from them would be a violation of the command and prohibition."

The response to this is that the verse and others that describe the angels as being in pure obedience without any absolute disobedience include both this world and the Hereafter. There is no reason to restrict their obligation to the Hereafter.

Furthermore, their "obligation" (تكليف) is not of the same genus as the obligation known in human society—meaning the commander (المكلف) attaching his will to the action of the subject (المكلف) in a conventional manner that entails reward and punishment in the context of choice and the possibility of obedience and disobedience. Rather, they are a creation among Allah's creations who possess pure, luminous essences; they do not will except what Allah wills, and they do not act except as they are commanded. The Exalted said: **بَلْ عِبادٌ مُكْرَمُونَ لا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ** — *Rather they are [His] honored servants. They do not venture to speak ahead of Him, and they act by His command.* (21:26-27). For this reason, there is no recompense for them for their deeds in terms of reward or punishment. They are obligated with an ontological obligation (تكليف تكويني) rather than a legislative one (تشريعي), differing according to their ranks. The Exalted said: **وَما مِنَّا إِلَّا لَهُ مَقامٌ مَعْلُومٌ** — *[The angels say,] ‘There is none among us but has a known station.’* (37:164). And He said of them: **وَما نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ لَهُ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِينا وَما خَلْفَنا** — *[Gabriel said,] ‘We do not descend except by the command of your Lord. To Him belongs whatever is before us and whatever is behind us...’* (19:64).

This noble verse, following the previous verses, is like a generalization after a specification. When the Exalted disciplined the wives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) by clarifying the evil effect of their distressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), He generalized the address, calling upon the believers as a whole to discipline themselves and their families and protect them from the Fire whose fuel is the very souls of those who enter it. That is, their evil deeds adhere to them and turn into a fire that punishes them, from which there is no salvation and no escape.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لا تَعْتَذِرُوا الْيَوْمَ إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — *O you who faithless! Do not make any excuses today. You are only being recompensed for what you used to do.* (66:7). This is a general address to the disbelievers after they have been recompensed with the Fire. They will offer excuses for their disbelief and sins, and they will be addressed: "Do not make excuses today"—which is the Day of Recompense—"you are only being recompensed for the very things you used to do." That is, the punishment with which you are being punished is your own evil deed that you performed, and today its reality has become manifest to you. Since you performed it, it has become necessary that you performed it; reality does not change, and the word of punishment that has come true against you will not turn into falsehood. This is the apparent meaning of the address.

It has been said: The meaning is: Do not make excuses—today—after entering the Fire, for making excuses is repentance (التوبة), and repentance is not accepted after entering the Fire. You are only being recompensed with what is necessary in exchange for your deed according to wisdom.

In following the previous verses with what is in this verse of the address of subjugation (خطاب القهر), there is an implicit threat and an indication that disobedience to Allah and His Messenger might lead to disbelief (الكفر).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا تُوبُوا إِلَى اللهِ تَوْبَةً نَصُوحاً عَسى رَبُّكُمْ أَنْ يُكَفِّرَ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ وَيُدْخِلَكُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ** — *O you who have faith! Repent to Allah with a sincere repentance! Maybe your Lord will absolve you of your misdeeds and admit you into gardens with streams running in them...* (66:8). **النصح** (sincerity/counsel) is seeking to perform an action or say a word that contains the well-being of its object. It also comes with the meaning of "purity" (الإخلاص), such as: "I was sincere (نصحت) to him in affection," meaning I made it pure—as mentioned by Al-Raghib. Thus, "sincere repentance" (التوبة النصوح) is that which turns its practitioner away from returning to the sin, or that which makes the servant pure in returning from the sin so that he does not return to what he repented from.

When He commanded the believers to protect themselves and their families from the Fire, He commanded them all a second time to repent, and He made the hope that Allah would cover their misdeeds and admit them into gardens with streams running in them contingent upon it.

His saying: **يَوْمَ لا يُخْزِي اللهُ النَّبِيَّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ** (*...on the day Allah will not let down the Prophet and those who have faith with him*). Al-Raghib said: "It is said: **خزي** (he was disgraced/shamed) ... when a brokenness (انكسار) affects him, either from himself or from another. That which affects him from himself is excessive bashfulness (الحياء), and its verbal noun is *al-khazayah*. That which affects him from another and is considered a type of belittlement (الاستخفاف), its verbal noun is *al-khizy*. *Al-ikhza'* (disgracing) comes from both *al-khazayah* and *al-khizy*." He said: "Similar to what we said regarding *khizy* are *dhull* (abasement) and *hawn* (humiliation). When these come from the person himself, it is called *al-hawn*—with a *fatha* on the *ha'*—and *al-dhull*, and it can be praiseworthy. When it comes from another, it is called *al-hawn*—with a *damma* on the *ha'*—and *al-hawan* and *al-dhull*, and it is blameworthy." [End of summary].

Thus, His saying: **يَوْمَ** (*on the day*) is an adverbial qualifier for what preceded it. The meaning is: Repent to Allah so that He may absolve you of your misdeeds and admit you into Paradise on a day when Allah will not disgrace or break the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) by making them deprived of dignity or by failing to fulfill the beautiful promise He made to them.

In His saying: **النَّبِيَّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ** (*the Prophet and those who have faith with him*), there is a consideration of "accompaniment" (المعية) in faith in this world, which necessitates their adhering to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and their obedience to him without opposition or contention.

It is possible that His saying: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (*those who have faith*) is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is "with him" (مَعَهُ), and His saying: **نُورُهُمْ يَسْعى** (*their light moving*) etc., is a second predicate, and His saying: **يَقُولُونَ** (*they say*) etc., is a third predicate. This would imply that they do not part from the Prophet, nor does he part from them on the Day of Resurrection. This is a good interpretation, the implication of which is that the absence of disgrace is specific to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), while the moving of the light and the request for its completion are specific to the believers who are with him. This is supported by the verse in Surah al-Hadid that follows. It is also possible that "with him" (مَعَهُ) is connected to His saying "they believed" (آمَنُوا), and His saying "their light moving" etc., are the first and second predicates for the relative pronoun.

His saying: **يَسْعى نُورُهُمْ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمانِهِمْ** (*their light moving before them and to their right*): Some discussion regarding its meaning preceded in His saying, the Exalted: **يَوْمَ تَرَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ يَسْعى نُورُهُمْ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمانِهِمْ** — *The day you will see the faithful, men and women, with their light moving before them and to their right...* (57:12). It is not unlikely that the light before them is the light of faith (الإيمان) and the light to their right is the light of deeds (العمل).

His saying: **يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا أَتْمِمْ لَنا نُورَنا وَاغْفِرْ لَنا إِنَّكَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** (*they say, ‘Our Lord! Perfect our light for us, and forgive us! Indeed You have power over all things’*). The context implies that the requested forgiveness (المغفرة) is a cause for the completion of the light, or it is concomitant with the completion of the light. This implies that there is a deficiency in their light. The light is the light of faith and deeds; thus, they have deficiencies according to the degrees of faith or the traces of misdeeds whose places in their records of servitude in action have remained empty. So they ask their Lord to complete their light for them and forgive them. To this, there is an indication in His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الصِّدِّيقُونَ وَالشُّهَداءُ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ وَنُورُهُمْ** — *Those who believe in Allah and His apostles—it is they who are the truthful and the witnesses with their Lord; they shall have their reward and their light.* (57:19).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ جاهِدِ الْكُفَّارَ وَالْمُنافِقِينَ وَاغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَمَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ** — *O Prophet! Wage jihad against the faithless and the hypocrites, and be harsh with them. Their abode shall be hell, and it is an evil destination.* (66:9). What is meant by "jihad" (الجهاد) is exerting effort in rectifying the matter from their side and repelling their evil. Regarding the disbelievers, it is by clarifying the truth and conveying it; if they believe, then well and good, otherwise it is war. Regarding the hypocrites, it is by winning them over and reconciling their hearts until their hearts find tranquility in faith; otherwise, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) never fought a hypocrite.

It has been said: What is meant is to be severe with them in establishing the legal punishments (الحدود), because most of those who were subject to the punishments in that time were the hypocrites. Both views are as you see.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, with its chain of narration from Ibn Sayyar, from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) regarding His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكَ ـ تَبْتَغِي مَرْضاتَ أَزْواجِكَ** — He (عليه السلام) said: "Aisha and Hafsa came upon the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while he was with Mariya. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'By Allah, I shall not go near her.' So Allah commanded him to expiate for his oath."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Zurara, from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام), who said: "I asked him about a man who says to his wife: 'You are forbidden (حرام) to me.' He said: 'If I had authority over him, I would make his head ache and say: Allah made her lawful for you, so what made her forbidden to you? He has done nothing more than lie, claiming that what Allah made lawful for him is forbidden. Neither divorce nor expiation applies to him.'"

I said: "The saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكَ** — so He placed an expiation in it?"

He said: "He only prohibited for himself his slave-girl Mariya the Copt—and he swore an oath not to go near her. The expiation was only placed upon the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) for the oath—it was not placed upon him for the prohibition."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated with a sound chain from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to drink a drink—of honey at Sawda's place. He entered upon Aisha and she said: 'I find a smell from you.' He entered upon Hafsa and she said: 'I find a smell from you.' He said: 'I think it is from a drink I drank at Sawda's—by Allah, I shall not drink it.' So Allah revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكَ**."

**I say:** The tradition is narrated through scattered chains and with different wordings, and their correspondence with the verses—which have a single context—is obscure.

In the same source, Ibn Sa'd and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Aisha and Hafsa were fond of each other. Hafsa went to her father's house to talk there. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sent for his slave-girl and she stayed with him in Hafsa's house—it was the day he was to go to Aisha. She found them in her house and began waiting for her to come out, becoming intensely jealous. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sent out his slave-girl and Hafsa entered. She said: 'I have seen who was with you; by Allah, you have grieved me.' The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'By Allah, I shall please you, and I am confiding a secret to you, so keep it.' She said: 'What is it?' He said: 'I call you to witness that this concubine of mine is forbidden to me, to please you.'"

"Hafsa went to Aisha and confided to her: 'Rejoice! The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) has forbidden his girl to himself.' When she informed of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) secret, Allah manifested it to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). So Allah revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكَ**."

**I say:** The correspondence of what is in the tradition with the verses, especially His saying: "He made known part of it and passed over part of it," is obscure.

In the same source, al-Tabarani and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: **وَإِذْ أَسَرَّ النَّبِيُّ إِلى بَعْضِ أَزْواجِهِ حَدِيثاً** — He said: "Hafsa entered upon the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in her house while he was having intercourse with Mariya. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to her: 'Do not tell Aisha, so that I may give you glad tidings—for your father shall take charge of the matter after Abu Bakr when I die.'"

"Hafsa went and told Aisha. Aisha said to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): 'Who informed you of this?' He said: 'The All-Knowing, the All-Aware informed me.' Aisha said: 'I will not look at you until you forbid Mariya.' So he forbade her. Then Allah revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ**."

**I say:** The reports in this chapter are many despite the differences among them. In most of them, he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) forbade Mariya to himself because of Hafsa's words, not Aisha's, and the one who said to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): **مَنْ أَنْبَأَكَ هذا** (*Who informed you of this?*) was Hafsa, meaning: "Who told you that I spread the secret?" rather than Aisha.

Despite this, they do not remove the vagueness of His saying, the Exalted: **عَرَّفَ بَعْضَهُ وَأَعْرَضَ عَنْ بَعْضٍ** (*he made known part of it and passed over part of it*). Yes, in what Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ali (عليه السلام), he said: "A noble person never seeks full disclosure, for Allah says: **عَرَّفَ بَعْضَهُ وَأَعْرَضَ عَنْ بَعْضٍ**." It is narrated from Abu Hatim from Mujahid, and Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn Abbas: "That which he made known was the matter of Mariya, and that which he passed over was his saying: 'Your father and her father shall take charge of the people after me,' for fear that it would spread."

The objection to this is: What is the aspect of "nobility" (الكرم) in his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) making known what he said regarding the prohibition of Mariya while passing over what he told her regarding their leadership, when the opposite would be more appropriate and closer to the mark?

Several chains have been narrated from Umar ibn al-Khattab regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) of the verses, but he did not mention that.

In several collections of traditions, including al-Bukhari, Muslim, and al-Tirmidhi, from Ibn Abbas, who said: "I remained eager to ask Umar about the two women from the Prophet's wives regarding whom Allah said: **إِنْ تَتُوبا إِلَى اللهِ فَقَدْ صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُما** — until Umar performed the Hajj and I performed it with him. When he was on part of the road, Umar turned aside and I turned with him with the water-skin. He relieved himself, then came, and I poured water on his hands and he performed ablution."

"I said: 'O Commander of the Faithful! Who are the two women from the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wives regarding whom Allah said: **إِنْ تَتُوبا إِلَى اللهِ فَقَدْ صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُما**?' He said: 'I am surprised at you, O Ibn Abbas! They are Aisha and Hafsa.' Then he began to narrate to me."

"He said: 'We, the people of Quraysh, used to dominate women. When we came to Medina, we found a people whose women dominated them. Our women began to learn from their women. I became angry with my wife one day, and she talked back to me. I disliked her talking back to me, so she said: Why do you dislike that? By Allah, the wives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) talk back to him, and one of them might desert him from the day until the night.' I said: 'She who does that among them has failed and lost.'"

"He said: 'My house was in al-Awali, and I had a neighbor from the Ansar. We used to take turns descending to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). He would descend one day and bring me the news of the revelation and other things, and I would descend one day and bring him the like of that.'"

"He said: 'We used to talk that Ghassan were shoeing their horses to raid us. He came one day and knocked on the door, so I went out to him. He said: A great matter has happened. I said: Has Ghassan come? He said: Greater than that! The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) has divorced his wives. I said to myself: Hafsa has failed and lost; I used to think that this would happen. When we prayed the morning prayer, I put on my clothes, then I went until I entered upon Hafsa, and she was crying. I said: Has the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) divorced you all? She said: I do not know; he is there, withdrawing in the upper room. I went and came to a black servant and said: Ask permission for Umar. He entered, then came out to me and said: I mentioned you to him, but he said nothing. I went to the mosque, and around the mosque were a group of people crying, so I sat with them.'"

"Then what I felt overcame me, so I went and came to the servant and said: Ask permission for Umar. He entered, then came out and said: I mentioned you to him, but he said nothing. I turned to go, but the servant called me and said: Enter, for he has given you permission. I entered, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was reclining on a reed mat; I saw its mark on his side. I said: O Messenger of Allah, have you divorced your wives? He said: No. I said: Allah is the Greatest! If you could have seen us, O Messenger of Allah—we, the people of Quraysh, used to dominate women. When we came to Medina, we found a people whose women dominated them. Our women began to learn from their women. I became angry one day with my wife, and she talked back to me. I disliked that, so she said: Why do you dislike it?"

"By Allah, the wives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) talk back to him, and one of them might desert him from the day until the night. I said: She who does that among them has failed. I entered upon Hafsa and said: Does one of you talk back to the Messenger of Allah and desert him from the day until the night? She said: Yes. I said: She who does that among you has failed and lost. Does one of you feel secure that Allah might become angry with her because of the anger of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and thus she would perish? The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) smiled."

"I said to Hafsa: Do not talk back to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), nor ask him for anything; ask me for whatever occurs to you. And do not let it deceive you if your neighbor is more beautiful than you and more beloved to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—he smiled again."

"I said: O Messenger of Allah, may I feel at ease? He said: Yes. I raised my head and saw nothing in the house except three hides. I said: O Messenger of Allah, supplicate to Allah to expand for your community, for He has expanded for Persia and Rome while they do not worship Allah. He sat up straight and said: Are you in doubt, O Ibn al-Khattab? Those are a people for whom their good things have been hastened in the life of this world. He had sworn not to enter upon his wives for a month, so Allah admonished him for that and ordained for him the expiation of the oath."

**I say:** This meaning is narrated from him in detail and in brief through various chains. The narration—as you see—does not mention what the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) confided to one of his wives. What was the "part of the news" that he made known and what was the part he passed over, which has such significance?

Furthermore, it is apparent in the narration that what is meant by "prohibition" in the verse is the prohibition of all his wives, but that does not correspond with the verse, which says: **لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكَ تَبْتَغِي مَرْضاتَ أَزْواجِكَ** (*Why do you prohibit what Allah has made lawful for you, seeking to please your wives?*). Additionally, it does not clarify the reason for the specification in His saying: **إِنْ تَتُوبا إِلَى اللهِ فَقَدْ صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُما وَإِنْ تَظاهَرا عَلَيْهِ** (*If the two of you repent to Allah... for your hearts have certainly swerved, and if you back each other against him*) etc.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, with its chain of narration from Abu Basir, who said: "I heard Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) say: **إِنْ تَتُوبا إِلَى اللهِ فَقَدْ صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُما وَإِنْ تَظاهَرا عَلَيْهِ فَإِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ مَوْلاهُ وَجِبْرِيلُ وَصالِحُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — He said: 'The righteous among the faithful (صالِحُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ) is Ali (عليه السلام).'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Asma' bint Umays: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) say regarding **وَصالِحُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ**: 'It is Ali ibn Abi Talib.'"

**I say:** The author of *al-Burhan*, after citing the previous narration of Abu Basir, mentioned that Muhammad ibn al-Abbas cited fifty-two traditions in this regard from both Shi'i and Sunni chains, then he cited a portion of them.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Abd al-A'la, the client of the family of Sam, from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "When this verse was revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا قُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ وَأَهْلِيكُمْ ناراً** (*O you who have faith! Save yourselves and your families from a Fire...*), a man from the believers sat crying and said: 'I have been unable to save myself, yet I have been burdened with my family.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'It is sufficient for you to command them with what you command yourself, and forbid them from what you forbid yourself.'"

In the same source, with its chain of narration from Sama'a, from Abu Basir regarding His saying: **قُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ وَأَهْلِيكُمْ ناراً** — I said: "How do I protect them?" He said: "You command them with what Allah has commanded, and you forbid them from what Allah has forbidden. If they obey you, you have protected them; and if they disobey you, you have fulfilled what was upon you."

**I say:** It was narrated through another chain from Dhur'a from Abu Basir from him (عليه السلام).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abd al-Razzaq, al-Faryabi, Sa'id ibn Mansur, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Madkhal* narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) regarding His saying: **قُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ وَأَهْلِيكُمْ ناراً** — He said: "Teach yourselves and your families good, and discipline them."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Zayd ibn Aslam, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited this verse: **قُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ وَأَهْلِيكُمْ ناراً**. They said: 'O Messenger of Allah, how do we protect our families from the Fire?' He said: 'You command them with what Allah loves and forbid them from what Allah dislikes.'"

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Abu al-Sabbah al-Kinani, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا تُوبُوا إِلَى اللهِ تَوْبَةً نَصُوحاً** — He said: 'The servant repents from the sin and then does not return to it.'"

Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl said: "I asked Abu al-Hasan (عليه السلام) about it, and he said: 'He repents from the sin and then does not return to it.'" [The tradition].

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Mu'adh ibn Jabal said: 'O Messenger of Allah, what is sincere repentance (التوبة النصوح)?' He said: 'That the servant regrets the sin he has committed, then he apologizes to Allah and does not return to it, just as the milk does not return to the udder.'"

**I say:** The narrations with this meaning are many from both sides.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Salih ibn Sahl al-Hamdani, who said: "Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) said regarding His saying: **يَسْعى نُورُهُمْ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمانِهِمْ** — 'The Imams of the believers on the Day of Resurrection shall move before the believers and to their right until they settle them in the stations of the people of Paradise.'"

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) regarding the verse: "Whoever has light on that day shall be saved, and every believer has a light."
